title: 🎙️ Cariboo Local Affairs
description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Cariboo Voices & Local News' — stories FROM and ABOUT the Cariboo-Chilcotin region and its communities. Score 80-100 for articles directly covering: news and events in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, Horsefly, Likely, Anahim Lake, Alexis Creek, or the Cariboo Regional District; local government and civic decisions (City of Williams Lake, City of Quesnel, CRD, District of 100 Mile House); local health services and emergency closures; local economy, businesses, and community programs; Cariboo arts, culture, and community events; AND digital equity, rural internet access (fiber, fixed wireless, Starlink), and connectivity serving these specific communities. Articles from local Cariboo news sources (mycariboonow.com, wltribune.com, quesnelobserver.com, 100milefreepress.net, cfjctoday.com) covering Cariboo community life should score 65-85. Score 50-79 for: BC provincial news directly affecting Cariboo communities, rural Canadian community stories closely paralleling Cariboo life, digital equity and rural internet broadly. Score 20-49 for: general BC news that may affect rural areas, broader rural Canada topics without specific Cariboo tie-in. Score 0-19 for: national/international news without Cariboo relevance, pure tech or entertainment with no community angle. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no Cariboo tie-in; science discoveries without a local community connection; consumer tech reviews and gadget guides; wildlife and conservation stories not involving Cariboo communities or First Nations; BC provincial news without direct Cariboo-region relevance. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.content_html:

## Garden and Art Tour adds colourful fun in Williams Lake Published 12:33 pm Sunday, July 12, 2026 **By rebeccadyok** 1/10 Debra and Bob McNie on Pigeon Avenue were featured in this year's Williams Lake Garden and Art Tour. (Rebecca Dyok/Williams Lake Tribune) The weather held up in Williams Lake on Saturday, July 11 for a self-guided garden tour of local landscapes throughout the area complete with live music and local artwork. (BC Wildfire Service) A wildfire located 1.5 kilometres west of the Fraser River continues to grow. According to BC Wildfire Service (BCWS), the French Bar Creek fire (K70748) was first discovered on July 4 and the cause remains under investigation. items.title: [Boing Boing] Castro's supercow got a state obituary and a marble statue
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

On a single day in January 1982, a Cuban cow named Ubre Blanca ("White Udder") produced 109.5 liters of milk — "more than four times a typical cow's production," according to Wikipedia. Guinness World Records recognized that mark, along with her 24,268.9-liter output in a single lactation period. — Read the rest

The post Castro's supercow got a state obituary and a marble statue appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] A bison tossed a Yellowstone tourist 8 feet in the air
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

A man walking with his grandson at Yellowstone's Bridge Bay Campground was hurled about eight feet into the air by a bull bison on July 10, Cowboy State Daily reports. The two believed they were keeping a safe distance — more than 100 yards — when a passing white pickup truck spooked the animal into charging, and it turned on the pair as they ducked into nearby trees. — Read the rest

The post A bison tossed a Yellowstone tourist 8 feet in the air appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] A company tried to make Monopoly in America and couldn't get dice
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

When the WS Game Company got hit with a seven-figure tariff bill on the board games it imports from China, CEO Jonathan Silva decided to find out whether he could make one in America instead. He picked a 250th-birthday edition called Monopoly: The Americana Edition and, according to NPR, hit a wall almost immediately: he couldn't find anyone in the United States to make him 10,000 dice. — Read the rest

The post A company tried to make Monopoly in America and couldn't get dice appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.content_html:

Researchers say future lunar missions should consider new ways to reduce and monitor this pollution before it becomes widespread.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Why do beaches today have seashells from clams and snails instead of brachiopods? A new study suggests the answer lies in Earth's greatest mass extinction, when warming oceans and falling oxygen levels wiped out animals that couldn't adapt. Species with body plans and metabolisms better suited to the changing conditions survived and went on to dominate the seas, offering a glimpse of how modern marine life could respond to climate change.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Why the human body has so many design flaws
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Many of the body's biggest flaws are the result of evolution building on old designs instead of starting over. Our spine, eyes, teeth, pelvis, and even certain nerves all reveal compromises that worked well enough for survival but still leave us prone to pain, injury, and disease. Structures like the appendix and ear muscles also remain because they were never harmful enough for evolution to eliminate. Together, these features tell the story of a body shaped by history rather than perfection.
items.summary: Many of the body's biggest flaws are the result of evolution building on old designs instead of starting over. items.title: [The Marginalian] Do Not Spare Yourself
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

The only thing more dangerous than wanting to save another person — a dangerous desire too often mistaken for love — is wanting to save yourself, to spare yourself the disappointment and heartbreak and loss inseparable from being a creature with hopes and longings constantly colliding with reality, with the indifference of time and chance, with the opposing hopes and longings of others. We have, of course, always invented institutions of salvation — religion to save us from our sins, therapy to save us from our traumas, marriage to save us from our loneliness — in order to salve our… read article
items.summary: The only thing more dangerous than wanting to save another person — a dangerous desire too often mistaken for love — is wanting to save yourself, to spare yourself the disappointment and heartbreak and loss inseparable from being a creature with hopes and longings constantly colliding with reality,
items._excerpt: The only thing more dangerous than wanting to save another person — a dangerous desire too often mistaken for love — is wanting to save yourself, to spare yourself the disappointment and heartbreak and loss inseparable from being a creature with hopes and longings constantly colliding with reality, with the indifference of time and chance, with the opposing hopes and longings of others. We have, of course, always invented institutions of salvation — religion to save us from our sins, therapy to save us from our traumas, marriage to save us from our loneliness — in order to salve our… read
